***30 Days of Night*** is a 2007 American [horror film](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") based on [the 2002 comic book miniseries of the same name](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night "30 Days of Night") by [Steve Niles](/wiki/Steve_Niles "Steve Niles"), who also co-wrote the film's screenplay. Directed by [David Slade](/wiki/David_Slade "David Slade") and starring [Josh Hartnett](/wiki/Josh_Hartnett "Josh Hartnett") and [Melissa George](/wiki/Melissa_George "Melissa George"), the story focuses on an Alaskan town beset by vampires as it enters into a 30-day-long [polar night](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night").

The film was released by [Sony Pictures Releasing](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Releasing "Sony Pictures Releasing") on October 19, 2007 to mixed critical reviews, and grossed $75.5 million against a budget of $30 million. Its success resulted in a [franchise](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night_(franchise) "30 Days of Night (franchise)"), including a two television [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries"), *[Blood Trails](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Blood_Trails "30 Days of Night: Blood Trails")* and *[Dust to Dust](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Dust_to_Dust "30 Days of Night: Dust to Dust")*, and a sequel film, *[Dark Days](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Dark_Days "30 Days of Night: Dark Days")*.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

The town of [Barrow, Alaska](/wiki/Barrow,_Alaska "Barrow, Alaska") prepares for the annual month-long [polar night](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night"). A stranger arrives from a large ship and sabotages the town's communications and transports. As Barrow's sheriff, Eben Oleson, investigates the sabotage, he learns that his estranged wife, Stella, missed the last plane out of town and must stay for the month.

That night, a band of [vampires](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire") attacks and slaughters most of the townspeople, including Eben's grandmother Helen. Eben, Stella and his brother Jake, along with seven other survivors, evade the vampires and take shelter in a boarded-up house with a hidden attic. In the police station holding cell, Marlow, the leader of the band, finds the stranger, who believes the vampires will "turn" him as a reward for his help. However, Marlow snaps his neck.

When the vampires torture a survivor to bait out the rest, Eben takes the opportunity to save a resident named John Riis, who was hiding underneath a hut. However, Eben is horrified to see that John has turned and reluctantly kills him in self-defense. Meanwhile, Isaac Bulosan, who has dementia, leaves the attic. As his son Wilson runs out into the open after him, both are killed.

Eighteen days later, a blizzard hits the town, causing a [whiteout](/wiki/Whiteout_(weather) "Whiteout (weather)"). The group goes to the general store for supplies but gets stranded after the blizzard abruptly ends. They head for the station while Eben creates a diversion. He lures the vampires to his grandmother's greenhouse and uses an ultraviolet light fixture to severely burn Marlow's lover, Iris, forcing him to mercy-kill her. Eben is aided by Beau Brower, the town snowplow operator, who kills many vampires with his tractor. When he crashes, Beau attempts a [suicide bombing](/wiki/Suicide_attack "Suicide attack") to kill the remaining vampires. However, Beau and the vampires survive the explosion, and Marlow crushes his head.

On Day 27, Stella and Eben see the deputy, Billy Kitka, signaling them with a flashlight and bring him back to the station after finding out he killed his family to save them from a more painful death. The trio find the others have made for the [utilidor](/wiki/Utility_tunnel "Utility tunnel"), a power and sewage treatment station that still has power, and head over there. Stella splits from the group to save Gail Robbins, a young girl whose family was slaughtered. Eben and Billy make it to the utilidor but are attacked by the vampire Arvin. Billy kills Arvin but gets bitten in the process. Eben is forced to kill him when he starts to turn.

As the month ends, with the sun due to rise, the vampires prepare to ignite the fuel line running through the town, planning to use the explosion to destroy evidence of their presence. Realizing Stella and Gail are trapped in the open and that he cannot beat the vampires in his current state, Eben injects himself with Billy's blood. Now a vampire, Eben confronts Marlow and manages to kill the vampire leader after a vicious fight, causing the remaining vampires to flee. With the attack over, Eben and Stella, now among the town's only survivors, watch the sunrise together, sharing one last kiss. As the sun rises, Eben's body burns to ash in Stella's arms.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

*30 Days of Night* author [Steve Niles](/wiki/Steve_Niles "Steve Niles") conceived of the story in the form of a comic, but after meeting a lack of interest in initial pitches, tried to pitch it as a film. When this did not work out, Niles shelved the idea until he showed it to [IDW Publishing](/wiki/IDW_Publishing "IDW Publishing"). IDW published the comic and [Ben Templesmith](/wiki/Ben_Templesmith "Ben Templesmith") provided the artwork.[[3]](#cite_note-3) When Niles and his agent, Jon Levin, shopped the comic around again as a potential film adaptation, Niles found that the idea "went shockingly well", with [Sam Raimi](/wiki/Sam_Raimi "Sam Raimi") and Senator International picking up the property rights based on the original concept and Templesmith's unique mood and concepts for the vampires.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

According to Raimi, the potential project was "unlike the horror films of recent years".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Following the publication of the [*30 Days of Night* comic book](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night "30 Days of Night") [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") in 2002, studios, including [DreamWorks](/wiki/DreamWorks_Pictures "DreamWorks Pictures"), [MGM](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"), and Senator International, bid in the $1 million range for rights to a potential vampire film based on the story. Raimi expressed interest in adapting the miniseries and was negotiating a production deal with his producing partner [Robert Tapert](/wiki/Robert_Tapert "Robert Tapert") to establish a label with Senator Entertainment, of which Senator International is the sales division.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In July 2002, Senator International acquired the rights for *30 Days of Night* in a seven-figure deal with Raimi and Tapert attached as producers.

By October 2002, Niles was working on adapting *30 Days of Night* for the big screen, keeping the film true to the miniseries, though fleshing out the characters more significantly in the adaptation process.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In February 2003, [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") partnered with Senator International to work on *30 Days of Night*, which was developing under Senator International's newly established production company, [Ghost House Pictures](/wiki/Ghost_House_Pictures "Ghost House Pictures"). [Mike Richardson](/wiki/Mike_Richardson_(publisher) "Mike Richardson (publisher)"), the [Dark Horse Comics](/wiki/Dark_Horse_Comics "Dark Horse Comics") publisher who supported the adaptation project from the beginning, after having turned down an offer to initially publish the project, was attached as [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer#Motion_pictures_and_television "Executive producer").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The following March, Richardson revealed that Steve Niles had turned in the initial draft for the *30 Days of Night* screenplay.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In March 2004, however, Columbia Pictures requested that Niles's initial screenplay to be rewritten in preparation for production.[[10]](#cite_note-bloodthirsty-10) Sue Binder, the business manager of Ghost House Pictures, indicated that filming for *30 Days of Night* was still at least a year away, as Ghost House planned to produce three films before the vampire thriller.[[10]](#cite_note-bloodthirsty-10) The following May, [Stuart Beattie](/wiki/Stuart_Beattie "Stuart Beattie"), one of the writers for *[Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_The_Curse_of_the_Black_Pearl "Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl")*, was rewriting Niles' *30 Days of Night* draft for production.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Niles was pleased with Beattie's faithfully rewritten script, which was submitted to the studio in October 2004.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Adi Hasak](/wiki/Adi_Hasak "Adi Hasak") also made uncredited contributions to the script.

In September 2005, director [David Slade](/wiki/David_Slade "David Slade") was announced to have signed on to *30 Days of Night*, which would be distributed by Columbia Pictures mainly in North America and [Mandate Pictures](/wiki/Mandate_Pictures "Mandate Pictures") in international territories.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In March 2006, Slade revealed that [screenwriter Brian Nelson](/wiki/Screenwriter_Brian_Nelson "Screenwriter Brian Nelson"), who wrote the screenplay for Slade's previous film *[Hard Candy](/wiki/Hard_Candy_(film) "Hard Candy (film)")*, was writing a new draft of the *30 Days of Night* script, replacing Beattie's draft.[[14]](#cite_note-shh-14) The director said that filming would begin in summer 2006 in Alaska and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").[[14]](#cite_note-shh-14)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Casting")]

In June 2006, [Josh Hartnett](/wiki/Josh_Hartnett "Josh Hartnett") was announced as having been cast as the husband of the married couple who serves as the town's sheriff team. Some criticized the choice because the main character (Eben Olemaun, with the last name changed to Oleson for the motion picture adaptation) was originally Inuk in the comics.[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Melissa George](/wiki/Melissa_George "Melissa George") joined the *30 Days of Night* cast as a sheriff and wife of Hartnett's character.[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Danny Huston](/wiki/Danny_Huston "Danny Huston") joined the cast as the leader of the vampires.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

Filming did not begin immediately, but in a September 2006 interview, executive producer Mike Richardson said that *30 Days of Night* would be shot on [35 mm film](/wiki/35mm_movie_film "35mm movie film"), though discussion had occurred to shoot the film on [Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_(Panavision) "Genesis (Panavision)").[[18]](#cite_note-18) In an interview prior to filming, Slade explained that the illustrations by Ben Templesmith would be reflected in production design. Slade also considered Nelson's draft to be the most faithful to the graphic novel. He also stated his intention to make a "scary vampire film", of which he did not think many existed. "The rest of them, they fall into all kinds of traps. We're going to try to do our best... and one of the ways we have to do it is be more naturalistic than the graphic novel, because it's very over-the-top," said Slade.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Also, concern was expressed that while the vampires needed to communicate, talking might lessen the effect. To counter this, a fictional vampire language, with [click consonants](/wiki/Click_consonant "Click consonant"), was constructed with the help of a professor of linguistics and the nearby University of Auckland. Slade explained, "we designed this really simple language that didn't sound like any particular accent that you would be aware of, that was based around really simple actions, eating, hunting, yes, no, really basic, because that's what vampires do."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-production")]

By February 2007, the production phase was completed, and a rough cut of the film was prepared.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In April, composer [Brian Reitzell](/wiki/Brian_Reitzell "Brian Reitzell") was hired to score the film.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Music")]

[Brian Reitzell](/wiki/Brian_Reitzell "Brian Reitzell") composed the [film's score](/wiki/Film_score "Film score"), with [Justin Meldal-Johnsen](/wiki/Justin_Meldal-Johnsen "Justin Meldal-Johnsen") performing. A soundtrack was released by [Invada Records](/wiki/Invada_Records "Invada Records"), with an artwork by Marc Bessant in summer 2015 on [vinyl](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Novelization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Novelization")]

To coincide with the film's release, a novelization by [Tim Lebbon](/wiki/Tim_Lebbon "Tim Lebbon") was published by Pocket Star on September 25, 2007.[[24]](#cite_note-24) It is one of six novels based on the franchise.

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Release")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Box office")]

*30 Days of Night* was released in 2,855 cinemas in the United States and Canada on October 19, 2007. In its opening weekend, the film grossed $16 million,[[2]](#cite_note-bom-2) placing first in the box office.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The film grossed $39.7 million in the United States and Canada and $35.7 million overseas for a total of $75.3 million worldwide.[[2]](#cite_note-bom-2)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

On the review aggregation site [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has an approval rating of 50% based on 156 reviews, with an average rating of 5.5/10. The site's critics consensus states: "While *30 Days of Night* offers a few thrills, it ultimately succumbs to erratic execution."[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") reports a weighted average score of 53 out of 100 based on 29 critics, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "C" on an A+ to F scale.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") gave the film 2.5 stars out of a possible 4. He criticized several [plot holes](/wiki/Plot_hole "Plot hole"), such as the vampires moving with supernatural speed in some attacks, but ponderous slowness in others, but also singled out Danny Huston for being "quite convincing" as the vampire leader, and summed up the film as "well-made, well-photographed, and plausibly acted, and is better than it needs to be."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Home media")]

*30 Days of Night* was released February 26, 2008 on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD"), [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc"), and [UMD](/wiki/Universal_Media_Disc "Universal Media Disc") for [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable") in the United States.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The video sold 1,386,716 million copies earning a profit of over $26,524,542 million.

## TV series, prequels, and sequel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=30_Days_of_Night_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: TV series, prequels, and sequel")]

A [prequel](/wiki/Prequel "Prequel") [miniseries](/wiki/Miniseries "Miniseries") *[Blood Trails](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Blood_Trails "30 Days of Night: Blood Trails")* was released in 2007.

A [sequel](/wiki/Sequel "Sequel") miniseries *[Dust to Dust](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Dust_to_Dust "30 Days of Night: Dust to Dust")* was released in 2008.

A [straight-to-DVD](/wiki/Direct-to-video "Direct-to-video") sequel entitled *[Dark Days](/wiki/30_Days_of_Night:_Dark_Days "30 Days of Night: Dark Days")* was released on October 5, 2010.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The script for the sequel was written by Steve Niles and Ben Ketai with Ketai also positioned as director. When filming began on October 20, 2009, [Rhys Coiro](/wiki/Rhys_Coiro "Rhys Coiro") and [Mia Kirshner](/wiki/Mia_Kirshner "Mia Kirshner") were named as leads, with Kirshner playing the lead vampire villain [Lilith](/wiki/Lilith_(30_Days_of_Night) "Lilith (30 Days of Night)").[[32]](#cite_note-32) Other cast named included [Harold Perrineau](/wiki/Harold_Perrineau "Harold Perrineau"), [Kiele Sanchez](/wiki/Kiele_Sanchez "Kiele Sanchez"), [Diora Baird](/wiki/Diora_Baird "Diora Baird"), and [Monique Ganderton](/wiki/Monique_Ganderton "Monique Ganderton").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Three days after filming began, Niles revealed that [Kiele Sanchez](/wiki/Kiele_Sanchez "Kiele Sanchez") replaced Melissa George in the role of [Stella Oleson](/wiki/Stella_Oleson "Stella Oleson").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The sequel was produced on a lower budget, but being straight-to-video allowed the writers to more closely follow the comic book.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
